27	// ChecksFile) via ApplyLandingToResult before the result is written to disk or
28	// returned to a caller.

34		ResultRev string `json:"result_rev,omitempty"`
35	
36		// Outcome and Status are initially set by the worker to task-level values
37		// (task_succeeded / task_failed / task_no_changes), then overwritten by
38		// ApplyLandingToResult with the landing decision (merged / preserved /
39		// no-changes) so callers see a unified record.
40		Outcome string `json:"outcome"`
41		Status  string `json:"status,omitempty"`
42		Detail  string `json:"detail,omitempty"`
43	
44		// Landing fields — populated by ApplyLandingToResult, not by ExecuteBead.

51		// ratchet thresholds were evaluated during landing. HELIX and other
52		// consumers use these to distinguish ratchet-preserved attempts from

117	// GitOps abstracts the git operations required by the worker.
118	// Merge is intentionally excluded — that belongs to the parent-side
119	// orchestrator (OrchestratorGitOps). UpdateRef/DeleteRef are exposed here
120	// so landing-side helpers (e.g. BuildLandingGateContext) can pin a
121	// transient ref while running gate evaluation against an ephemeral
122	// worktree.
123	type GitOps interface {
124		HeadRev(dir string) (string, error)
125		ResolveRev(dir, rev string) (string, error)
126		WorktreeAdd(dir, wtPath, rev string) error
127		WorktreeRemove(dir, wtPath string) error
128		WorktreeList(dir string) ([]string, error)
129		WorktreePrune(dir string) error
130		IsDirty(dir string) (bool, error)
131		// SynthesizeCommit stages real file changes (excluding harness noise paths) and
132		// commits them using msg as the commit message. Returns (true, nil) when a
133		// commit was made, (false, nil) when there was nothing real to commit (all
134		// dirty files were noise), and (false, err) on failure.
135		SynthesizeCommit(dir, msg string) (bool, error)
136		// UpdateRef updates ref in dir to sha. Used by landing helpers to pin a
137		// commit so a transient worktree can check it out without racing with
138		// other work that might prune it.
139		UpdateRef(dir, ref, sha string) error
140		// DeleteRef removes ref from dir. Used to unpin a transient ref after
141		// the consumer (e.g. an ephemeral worktree) is done with it.
142		DeleteRef(dir, ref string) error
143	}

40		cmd := &cobra.Command{

42			Short: "Run an agent on one bead in an isolated worktree, then land the result",
43			Long: `execute-bead is the primitive: it runs a single agent on a single bead in
44	an isolated git worktree (the worker step), then hands the result to the
45	parent-side orchestrator which merges or preserves the commits.
46	
47	The worker classifies its outcome as one of:
48	  task_succeeded  — agent exited 0 and produced commits
49	  task_no_changes — agent exited 0 but made no commits
50	  task_failed     — agent exited non-zero
51	
52	The orchestrator then applies landing logic (merge, gate evaluation, preserve)
53	and the final result reflects both the worker and landing decisions.
54	
55	For normal queue-driven work use "ddx agent execute-loop", which claims
56	ready beads, calls this command, and closes or unclaims each bead based on
57	the result. Reach for execute-bead directly only to debug or re-run a
58	specific bead.
59	
60	Result status is reported on the "status:" line and is one of:
61	  success                          — merged (or preserved with --no-merge)
62	  no_changes                       — agent ran but produced no diff
63	  no_evidence_produced             — agent exited with no commit and no rationale
64	  already_satisfied                — closed by the loop after repeated no_changes
65	  land_conflict                    — merge failed; result preserved
66	  post_run_check_failed            — checks failed; result preserved
67	  execution_failed                 — agent or harness error
68	  structural_validation_failed     — bead or prompt inputs invalid
69	
70	execute-loop closes the bead with session/commit evidence only on success
71	(and already_satisfied); every other status leaves the bead open and
72	unclaimed for a later attempt.`,
73			Example: `  # Debug one specific bead (prefer "execute-loop" for normal queue work)
74	  ddx agent execute-bead ddx-7a01ba6c
75	
76	  # Run against a non-HEAD base revision
77	  ddx agent execute-bead ddx-7a01ba6c --from main

27		// RoutePreflight, when non-nil, is invoked between nextCandidate and

29		// loop's resolved (harness, model) and return whatever typed routing
30		// error the upstream surfaced — notably agent.ErrHarnessModelIncompatible

32		// causes the loop to exit immediately without claiming the bead, with

35		// consumes the typed-incompatibility surface.

46	// DefaultReviewMaxRetries is the number of reviewer attempts allowed per
47	// committed result_rev before the loop emits a terminal
48	// `review-manual-required` event and stops re-executing primary. FEAT-022 §14.
49	const DefaultReviewMaxRetries = 3

51	// MaxLoopCooldown is the absolute upper bound the execute-loop will set for
52	// any execute-loop-retry-after value. Year-scale parks effectively mean
53	// "never retry" and that should be a deliberate operator choice via
54	// `ddx bead update --set execute-loop-retry-after=...`, not an automatic
55	// loop decision. Beyond this cap, the loop refuses to lengthen the cooldown
56	// further; an operator extending it manually still works.
57	const MaxLoopCooldown = 24 * time.Hour
58	
59	// LandConflictCooldown is the short cooldown applied when all conflict-recovery
60	// paths fail (3-way ort auto-merge + optional focused-resolve agent) and the
61	// bead is parked as land_conflict_unresolvable. Much shorter than
62	// MaxLoopCooldown because land conflicts often resolve quickly as sibling beads
63	// advance the base branch.
64	const LandConflictCooldown = 15 * time.Minute
65	
66	// StoreErrorCooldown is the short cooldown applied when a Store.* operation in
67	// the outcome-handling block returns a transient error. It prevents an
68	// immediate re-queue of the affected bead while allowing the loop to continue
69	// to the next candidate. Store errors during outcome handling are non-fatal:
70	// they are logged and emitted as loop-error events but do not terminate the
71	// worker.
72	const StoreErrorCooldown = 5 * time.Minute
73	
74	// handleOutcomeStoreError is called when a Store.* operation in the
75	// post-Execute outcome block returns a non-nil error. It logs the error,
76	// appends a kind:loop-error event to the bead (best-effort), sets a short
77	// cooldown so the bead is not immediately re-queued, increments
78	// result.Failures, and returns true — signalling the caller to continue to
79	// the next loop iteration rather than returning.
80	//
81	// If ctx is already cancelled, it returns false so the caller can propagate
82	// the cancellation immediately.
83	func (w *ExecuteBeadWorker) handleOutcomeStoreError(ctx context.Context, beadID, operation string, storeErr error, assignee string, result *ExecuteBeadLoopResult, runtime ExecuteBeadLoopRuntime, now func() time.Time) bool {
84		if ctx.Err() != nil {
85			return false
86		}
87		if runtime.Log != nil {
88			_, _ = fmt.Fprintf(runtime.Log, "outcome store error (%s %s): %v (continuing)\n", operation, beadID, storeErr)
89		}
90		_ = w.Store.AppendEvent(beadID, bead.BeadEvent{
91			Kind:      "loop-error",
92			Summary:   operation + " failed",
93			Body:      storeErr.Error(),
94			Actor:     assignee,
95			Source:    "ddx agent execute-loop",
96			CreatedAt: now().UTC(),
97		})
98		cooldownUntil := now().UTC().Add(StoreErrorCooldown)
99		_ = w.Store.SetExecutionCooldown(beadID, cooldownUntil, "loop-error", operation+": "+storeErr.Error())
100		result.Failures++
101		result.LastFailureStatus = "loop-error"
102		return true
103	}
104	
105	// capLoopCooldown clamps a loop-set cooldown duration to MaxLoopCooldown.
106	// The loop uses this for every SetExecutionCooldown call so no automatic
107	// path can silently park a bead for a year.
108	func capLoopCooldown(d time.Duration) time.Duration {
109		if d <= 0 {
110			return 0
111		}
112		if d > MaxLoopCooldown {
113			return MaxLoopCooldown
114		}
115		return d
116	}

26	// DefaultProviderRequestTimeout is the per-request wall-clock cap for
27	// standard (non-thinking) providers. It guards against a provider that
28	// emits headers but then stalls entirely — a scenario the idle-read
29	// timeout below catches for streaming providers, but which also applies
30	// to non-streaming Chat calls where no body bytes arrive at all.
31	//
32	// Relationship between the two timeouts:
33	//   - DefaultProviderIdleReadTimeout (5 min): fires when no stream delta
34	//     arrives for 5 continuous minutes. This is the primary defense against
35	//     stalled TCP sockets on streaming providers.
36	//   - DefaultProviderRequestTimeout (15 min): wall-clock cap on the entire
37	//     Chat/ChatStream call. This is a secondary backstop for non-streaming
38	//     paths and should NOT be the primary tool for limiting thinking models,
39	//     since those can legitimately spend >15 min on reasoning before the
40	//     first delta. Use DefaultThinkingModelProviderRequestTimeout (60 min)
41	//     for models known to do extended chain-of-thought reasoning.
42	//
43	// To override per-project, set agent.endpoints.<name>.request_timeout_seconds
44	// in .ddx/config.yaml, or pass --request-timeout DURATION to execute-bead /
45	// execute-loop for one-off debugging.
46	const DefaultProviderRequestTimeout = 15 * time.Minute
47	
48	// DefaultThinkingModelProviderRequestTimeout is the per-request wall-clock
49	// cap for known thinking / chain-of-thought reasoning models (e.g.
50	// qwen3.x, deepseek-r1, o1, o3). These models can spend 5–20+ minutes on
51	// internal reasoning before emitting the first response token, so the
52	// standard 15-minute cap would kill legitimate requests. The idle-read
53	// timeout still applies — a truly stalled stream fires after 5 min of
54	// silence regardless of this cap.
55	const DefaultThinkingModelProviderRequestTimeout = 60 * time.Minute
56	
57	// DefaultProviderIdleReadTimeout bounds the maximum idle gap between stream
58	// deltas. This is the primary stalled-TCP-socket defense: when no body bytes
59	// arrive for 5 continuous minutes the provider is assumed hung and the call
60	// fails. Thinking models that are actively streaming reasoning tokens will
61	// NOT hit this timeout — they only risk DefaultThinkingModelProviderRequestTimeout
62	// (60 min) if a single call exceeds the wall-clock cap.
63	const DefaultProviderIdleReadTimeout = 5 * time.Minute
64	
65	// thinkingModelPrefixes is the set of model-name substrings (lowercased)
66	// that identify thinking / chain-of-thought reasoning models requiring a
67	// longer per-request wall-clock cap. Extend this list when a new model
68	// class needs more than 15 minutes to produce its first response token.
69	var thinkingModelPrefixes = []string{
70		"qwen3",      // qwen3.x series (e.g. qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
71		"qwen-r1",    // Qwen R1 series
72		"deepseek-r", // deepseek-r1, deepseek-r2, deepseek-reasoner
73		"deepseek/r", // deepseek/r1 (openrouter format)
74		"o1-",        // OpenAI o1 series (o1-mini, o1-preview)
75		"o1-pro",     // OpenAI o1-pro
76		"o3",         // OpenAI o3 / o3-mini
77		"thinking",   // any model with "thinking" in the name
78	}
79	
80	// isThinkingModel reports whether model is a known thinking/reasoning
81	// model that requires a longer per-request timeout than the standard 15 min.
82	func isThinkingModel(model string) bool {
83		low := strings.ToLower(model)
84		for _, prefix := range thinkingModelPrefixes {
85			if strings.Contains(low, prefix) {
86				return true
87			}
88		}
89		return false
90	}
91	
92	// ResolveProviderRequestTimeout returns the effective wall-clock cap for a
93	// single Chat/ChatStream call. Resolution order:
94	//  1. override if > 0 (from --request-timeout CLI flag via ResolvedConfig)
95	//  2. agent.endpoints[n].request_timeout_seconds in .ddx/config.yaml for
96	//     the named provider endpoint
97	//  3. DefaultThinkingModelProviderRequestTimeout (60 min) when model is a
98	//     known thinking/reasoning model
99	//  4. DefaultProviderRequestTimeout (15 min) for all other models
100	//
101	// The idle-read timeout (DefaultProviderIdleReadTimeout, 5 min) is a
102	// separate mechanism and is NOT affected by this function.
103	func ResolveProviderRequestTimeout(workDir, providerName, model string, override time.Duration) time.Duration {
104		if override > 0 {
105			return override
106		}
107		if workDir != "" && providerName != "" {
108			if cfg, err := ddxconfig.LoadWithWorkingDir(workDir); err == nil {
109				if t := endpointRequestTimeout(cfg, providerName); t > 0 {
110					return t
111				}
112			}
113		}
114		if isThinkingModel(model) {
115			return DefaultThinkingModelProviderRequestTimeout
116		}
117		return DefaultProviderRequestTimeout
118	}

120	// endpointRequestTimeout looks up the request_timeout_seconds for the named
121	// provider in cfg.Agent.Endpoints. Returns 0 if not configured or not found.
122	func endpointRequestTimeout(cfg *ddxconfig.Config, providerName string) time.Duration {
123		if cfg == nil || cfg.Agent == nil {
124			return 0
125		}
126		for i, ep := range cfg.Agent.Endpoints {
127			if ep.RequestTimeoutSeconds <= 0 {
128				continue
129			}
130			name, _, err := endpointProviderEntry(ep, i)
131			if err != nil {
132				continue
133			}
134			if name == providerName {
135				return time.Duration(ep.RequestTimeoutSeconds) * time.Second
136			}
137		}
138		return 0
139	}

1600	func (f *CommandFactory) newAgentExecuteLoopCommand() *cobra.Command {
1601		cmd := &cobra.Command{
1602			Use:   "execute-loop",
1603			Short: "Drain the single-project execution-ready bead queue",
1604			Long: `execute-loop is the primary queue-driven execution surface. It scans the
1605	target project's execution-ready bead queue, claims the next ready bead,
1606	runs "ddx agent execute-bead" on it from the project root, records the
1607	structured result, and continues until no unattempted ready work remains.
1608	
1609	Reach for execute-loop by default. Use "ddx agent execute-bead" directly
1610	only as the primitive for debugging or re-running one specific bead.
1611	
1612	Planning and document-only beads are valid execution targets — any bead
1613	with unmet acceptance criteria and no blocking deps is eligible.
1614	
1615	Close semantics (per execute-bead result status):
1616	  success                      — close bead with session + commit evidence
1617	  already_satisfied            — close bead (after repeated no_changes)
1618	  no_changes                   — unclaim; may cooldown or close after retries
1619	  land_conflict                — unclaim; result preserved under refs/ddx/iterations/
1620	  post_run_check_failed        — unclaim; result preserved
1621	  execution_failed             — unclaim
1622	  structural_validation_failed — unclaim
1623	
1624	Only success (and already_satisfied) closes the bead. Every other status
1625	leaves the bead open and unclaimed so a later attempt can try again. Each
1626	attempt is appended to the bead as an execute-bead event (status, detail,
1627	base_rev, result_rev, preserve_ref, retry_after), and the underlying agent
1628	session log is recorded under the execute-bead agent-log path.
1629	
1630	By default execute-loop submits to the running ddx server as a background
1631	worker and returns immediately. Use --local to run inline in the current
1632	process.
1633	
1634	Project targeting (multi-project servers):
1635	  --project <path>    target a specific project root (absolute path or name)
1636	  DDX_PROJECT_ROOT    env var fallback; used when --project is not set
1637	  (default)           the git root of the current working directory
1638	
1639	When submitting to a multi-project server you must ensure the target project
1640	is registered with the server (run "ddx server" from that directory, or use
1641	"ddx server projects register"). The server rejects unrecognised project paths.
1642	`,
1643			Example: `  # Drain the current execution-ready queue once and exit (normal surface)
1644	  ddx agent execute-loop
1645	
1646	  # Pick one ready bead, execute it, and stop
1647	  ddx agent execute-loop --profile default --once
1648	
1649	  # Run continuously as a bounded queue worker
1650	  ddx agent execute-loop --poll-interval 30s

1652	  # Force a specific harness/model for a debugging pass

1655

## [v0.9.24] — 2026-04-28

Two harness-parity blockers surfaced by the first agent-vs-pi paired
beadbench run.

### Fixed

- **Pi-runner panic on `send on closed channel`** (`agent-195bb183`).
  The `mirroredEvents` goroutine in `internal/harnesses/pi/runner.go`
  was leaked: nothing closed the intermediate channel after
  `parsePiStream` returned, so an in-flight event could race
  `run()`'s deferred `close(out)` and panic. Fix: `mirroredEvents`
  now reports ownership of the mid-channel; the parser-goroutine
  closes it and waits for the mirror goroutine to drain before
  signaling done. Added `TestRunner_Execute_MirrorClosesCleanly`,
  passes 20× under `-race`.
- **Test-config isolation in `agent_test`** (`agent-27806ad5`). Tests
  calling `agent.New(ServiceOptions{})` auto-loaded the developer's
  live `~/.config/agent/config.yaml`, including provider types older
  base revisions didn't know about — making bead-verify-worktrees
  fail on `go test ./...` for reasons unrelated to the bead. New
  package-level `TestMain` (`service_testmain_test.go`) points
  `HOME` and `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` at a fresh tempdir before `m.Run()`.
  Per-test `t.Setenv("HOME", …)` calls still take precedence.

## [v0.9.23] — 2026-04-28

External-benchmark integration. First adapter to compare our harness
against the public scoreboard.

### Added

- **TerminalBench adapter** (`agent-6d6ae2f6`, PR #4). New
  `internal/benchmark/external/termbench/` package that translates
  TerminalBench task format → `agent.ServiceExecuteRequest` and
  captures `harnesses.Event` streams into ATIF v1.4 trajectory output
  the upstream verifier expects. Reads upstream `reward.txt` without
  reimplementing grading — the value of an external benchmark is
  using their rubric.
- Submodule at `scripts/benchmark/external/terminal-bench-2/` pinned
  to upstream commit `53ff2b8`. (Bead originally pointed at
  `laude-institute/terminal-bench`; that's stale per SD-008 — current
  dataset lives at terminal-bench-2.)
- Frozen 20-task subset at
  `scripts/beadbench/external/termbench-subset.json` (15 stratified
  hard/medium tasks reused from the evidence-grade comparison + 5
  easy smoke tasks).
- `cmd/bench --external=termbench` flag plus
  `cmd/bench/external_termbench.go` runner. Writes results to
  `benchmark-results/termbench/<run-id>/results.json`.
- `docs/helix/02-design/external-benchmarks.md` — pattern doc for
  adding more external benchmark adapters (SWE-bench, AgentBench,
  etc.) following the TerminalBench shape.
- `docs/research/external-benchmark-baseline-2026-04-27.md` —
  baseline matrix scaffold (3 tasks × 3 arms: ddx-agent on
  Qwen3.6-plus, codex on GPT-5.4, claude-code on Sonnet-4.6). Cells
  marked `_pending_` until the verifier-side run lands.

### Honestly-scoped v1 gaps

- The adapter does not invoke Harbor / pytest / Docker. It writes
  the artifacts the verifier expects; actual scoring remains
  `scripts/benchmark/run_benchmark.sh`'s job.
- Baseline doc cells are placeholders pending verifier-side run.

## [v0.9.22] — 2026-04-28

Refactor + reliability release. Closes the architectural smell that
caused v0.9.18-.20's lucebox/vllm shipping bug, lays down the
internal-benchmark-corpus foundation, and unblocks lucebox conformance
on thinking-mode locals.

### Refactor

- **Provider registry** (`agent-8e4eb44c`). Single source of truth for
  provider-type → factory mappings in `internal/provider/registry/`.
  Each provider package registers a Descriptor in `init()`. The two
  parallel factories (`internal/config/config.go:buildProviderFromConfig`
  and `service_native_provider.go:buildNativeProvider`) collapse to

  registered (anthropic, lmstudio, lucebox, minimax, ollama, omlx,
  openai, openrouter, qwen, vllm, zai). Round-trip test in
  `registry_test.go` enumerates expected types and asserts every one

9	// CLIOverrides carries per-invocation flag values that override
10	// project config. Zero values mean "no override; use config".
11	// Pointer fields distinguish "explicit value" from "not set".
12	type CLIOverrides struct {
13		Harness       string
14		Model         string
15		Provider      string
16		ModelRef      string
17		Profile       string

24		Assignee      string

26		// ProviderRequestTimeout, when non-nil, overrides the per-request wall-clock
27		// cap applied to a single Chat/ChatStream call. Corresponds to --request-timeout
28		// on execute-bead and execute-loop. Zero pointer means "use config or model default".
29		ProviderRequestTimeout *time.Duration
30	}
31	
32	// Resolve produces a sealed ResolvedConfig by layering overrides onto
33	// cfg. Overrides are applied as last-write-wins per field. The
34	// returned ResolvedConfig does not alias cfg's storage — every map
35	// and slice is deep-cloned via per-type Clone methods before being
36	// captured.
37	//
38	// Resolve accepts a nil cfg and returns a ResolvedConfig populated
39	// from package defaults, sealed normally.
40	func (c *NewConfig) Resolve(overrides CLIOverrides) ResolvedConfig {
41		r := ResolvedConfig{sealed: true}
42	
43		r.assignee = overrides.Assignee
44		r.reviewMaxRetries = c.ResolveReviewMaxRetries()
45	
46		var agent *AgentConfig
47		var workers *WorkersConfig
48		if c != nil {
49			agent = c.Agent.Clone()
50			workers = c.Workers
51		}
52	
53		r.noProgressCooldown = workers.ResolveNoProgressCooldown()
54		r.maxNoChangesBeforeClose = workers.ResolveMaxNoChangesBeforeClose()
55		r.heartbeatInterval = workers.ResolveHeartbeatInterval()
56	
57		r.harness = overrides.Harness
58		if r.harness == "" && agent != nil {
59			r.harness = agent.Harness
60		}
61	
62		r.model = overrides.Model
63		if r.model == "" && agent != nil {
64			r.model = agent.Model
65		}
66	
67		r.provider = overrides.Provider
68		r.modelRef = overrides.ModelRef
69		r.profile = overrides.Profile
70		r.minTier = overrides.MinTier
71		r.maxTier = overrides.MaxTier
72		r.effort = overrides.Effort
73	
74		r.permissions = overrides.Permissions
75		if r.permissions == "" && agent != nil {
76			r.permissions = agent.Permissions
77		}
78	
79		if overrides.Timeout != nil {
80			r.timeout = *overrides.Timeout
81		} else if agent != nil && agent.TimeoutMS > 0 {
82			r.timeout = time.Duration(agent.TimeoutMS) * time.Millisecond
83		}
84	
85		if overrides.ProviderRequestTimeout != nil {
86			r.providerRequestTimeout = *overrides.ProviderRequestTimeout
87		}
88	
89		r.evidenceCaps = c.ResolveEvidenceCaps(r.harness)
90	
91		r.contextBudget = overrides.ContextBudget
92		if r.contextBudget == "" && c != nil {
93			r.contextBudget = c.EvidenceCaps.ResolveContextBudget()
94		}
95	
96		if agent != nil {
97			r.sessionLogDir = agent.SessionLogDir
98			if agent.ReasoningLevels != nil {
99				r.reasoningLevels = make(map[string][]string, len(agent.ReasoningLevels))
100				for k, v := range agent.ReasoningLevels {
101					r.reasoningLevels[k] = append([]string(nil), v...)
102				}
103			}
104		}
105		if c != nil && c.Executions != nil && c.Executions.Mirror != nil {
106			r.mirrorConfig = c.Executions.Mirror.Clone()
107		}
108	
109		return r
110	}

114	// projectRoot, layers overrides on top, and returns the immutable
115	// ResolvedConfig.
116	//
117	// On load error, LoadAndResolve still returns a usable, sealed
118	// ResolvedConfig populated from package defaults plus the supplied
119	// overrides, alongside the underlying error. Callers decide whether to
120	// surface the error or proceed with the defaults-backed config.

data.output:
# Changelog

All notable changes to DDx are documented in this file.

## [Unreleased]

### Breaking changes

#### Removed: `agent.routing.default_harness`

The `agent.routing.default_harness` field has been removed from `.ddx/config.yaml`
(bead ddx-87fb72c2). DDx now refuses to load any config that still carries it
and prints a migration pointer instead.

Why: `default_harness` was a silent fallback that competed with the top-level
`agent.harness` setting and the live route-resolution call graph. It produced
confusing routing decisions when the upstream service had no viable provider for
the requested profile. The endpoint-first redesign (epic ddx-fdd3ea36) makes
harness selection an explicit decision per dispatch — a config-level "default"
no longer fits.

Migration: delete the field. The top-level `agent.harness` is still honored as
a tie-break preference on the resolution path (NOT a default override). See
[docs/migrations/routing-config.md](docs/migrations/routing-config.md).

#### Removed: `agent.routing.profile_ladders` and `agent.routing.model_overrides`

These fields and the `--escalate` / `--override-model` CLI flags are removed
(bead ddx-3bd7396a). DDx refuses to load any config that still carries them.

Migration: delete the fields. The default execute-loop path issues exactly one
`ResolveRoute` call per bead; tier-ladder iteration is gone. See
[docs/migrations/routing-config.md](docs/migrations/routing-config.md).

### Fix: `latency_ms` in claude harness traces now reflects per-call duration

Previously every `llm.response` event in `.ddx/agent-logs/agent-claude-*.jsonl`
had `latency_ms == elapsed_ms` because the claude stream parser wrote the
cumulative wall-clock value into both fields. Per-call LLM latency was
therefore unrecoverable from captured traces.

The parser now tracks the time the most recent LLM request was sent (session
start, then reset on each `user`/tool_result event) and emits
`latency_ms = now - lastRequestSentAt` for each assistant turn. `elapsed_ms`
remains cumulative, so for any event past turn 1 the two values diverge.

Legacy traces produced before this commit have `latency_ms == elapsed_ms` and
should be ignored by latency-analysis tooling for runs predating the fix.

Regression coverage: `TestParseClaudeStreamLatencyIsPerCall` in
no CHANGELOG.md in cli

## [Unreleased]

### Added: MinPower/MaxPower passthrough contract (agent v0.9.25)

DDx now consumes the upstream `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` power-bounds
interface introduced in FEAT-006 / CONTRACT-003.

**What changed:**

- `cli/go.mod` pins `agent v0.9.25`, which exposes `MinPower`/`MaxPower` on
  `ServiceExecuteRequest`, `Power int` on `ModelInfo`, and per-candidate
  `Components.Power` in `routing_decision` events.
- `--min-power` / `--max-power` flags added to `ddx agent execute-bead` and
  `ddx agent execute-loop` (alias `ddx work`). Values are passed to
  `ServiceExecuteRequest.MinPower/MaxPower` unchanged; DDx does not interpret
  them — the agent owns model selection within the bounds.
- `result.ActualPower` is now populated from the `routing_decision` event's
  winning candidate `Components.Power` and appears in `result.json` artifacts.
- `ddx agent models` now prints each model's power number when non-zero.
- `GET /api/agent/models` already returns `ModelInfo` which includes `Power`;
  no server-side change was required.

**No profile aliases or DDx-side route decisions are added.** The power bounds
are an opaque passthrough envelope between DDx and the agent's routing layer.

## [Unreleased]

input.new_string:
## [Unreleased]

### Added: MinPower/MaxPower passthrough contract (agent v0.9.25)

DDx now consumes the upstream `github.com/DocumentDrivenDX/agent` power-bounds
interface introduced in FEAT-006 / CONTRACT-003.

**What changed:**

- `cli/go.mod` pins `agent v0.9.25`, which exposes `MinPower`/`MaxPower` on
  `ServiceExecuteRequest`, `Power int` on `ModelInfo`, and per-candidate
  `Components.Power` in `routing_decision` events.
- `--min-power` / `--max-power` flags added to `ddx agent execute-bead` and
  `ddx agent execute-loop` (alias `ddx work`). Values are passed to
  `ServiceExecuteRequest.MinPower/MaxPower` unchanged; DDx does not interpret
  them — the agent owns model selection within the bounds.
- `result.ActualPower` is now populated from the `routing_decision` event's
  winning candidate `Components.Power` and appears in `result.json` artifacts.
- `ddx agent models` now prints each model's power number when non-zero.
- `GET /api/agent/models` already returns `ModelInfo` which includes `Power`;
  no server-side change was required.

**No profile aliases or DDx-side route decisions are added.** The power bounds
are an opaque passthrough envelope between DDx and the agent's routing layer.

### Breaking changes
